did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a deep neural network (DNN) based on a modified EfficientNetV2-M architecture for skin cancer detection and classification in dermoscopic images. The study involves training the model using transfer learning and fine-tuning on a combined dataset of dermoscopic images (ISIC 2018, 2019, 2020, and HAM10000) to perform binary and multiclass classification of skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a deep neural network (DNN) using a modified EfficientNetV2-M architecture for skin cancer detection. It details the construction of a knowledge base from combined datasets (ISIC 2018, 2019, 2020, HAM10000), describes the training methodology (transfer learning, fine-tuning, data augmentation), and reports new performance results (accuracy, AUC, F1 scores) comparing the proposed model against state-of-the-art methods. The structure includes standard research sections such as Introduction, Related Works, Research Contributions, Methods, Results, and Conclusion.

title: A deep neural network using modified EfficientNet for skin cancer detection in dermoscopic images
brief_summary: This study proposes a deep neural network using a modified EfficientNetV2-M architecture for the detection and classification of skin cancer in dermoscopic images. The model was trained on a combined knowledge base of 58,032 refined dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2018, 2019, 2020, and HAM10000 datasets. The authors employed transfer learning, fine-tuning, and data augmentation techniques to enhance model performance and prevent overfitting. The system was evaluated for both binary (malignant vs. benign) and multiclass (seven distinct classes) classification tasks. Results demonstrated that the modified EfficientNetV2-M outperformed state-of-the-art models, achieving an accuracy of 94.80% on the ISIC 2020 dataset and 95.49% on the ISIC 2019 dataset for multiclass classification. The study concludes that the proposed DNN serves as an effective assistive technology for computer-aided diagnosis in dermatology.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (malignant vs. benign) and multiclass classification (seven distinct classes: AK, BCC, BKL, MEL, NV, SCC, Unknown)
all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification by grouping the seven lesion classes into malignant (MEL, BCC, SCC) and benign (AK, BKL, NV, Unknown) categories. It also performs multiclass classification to identify specific lesion types. The primary task is classification as the model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using accuracy, AUC, F1 score, precision, and recall metrics.

datasets_used.name: Combined Knowledge Base (ISIC 2020, ISIC 2019, ISIC 2018, HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive knowledge base constructed by amalgamating dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2020, ISIC 2019, and ISIC 2018 datasets, along with the HAM10000 dataset. The images were refined following data-centric strategies. The combined dataset contains 58,032 images.

use.training: Used to train the modified EfficientNetV2-M and EfficientNet-B4 models using transfer learning and fine-tuning.
use.testing: The combined dataset was used for training; specific subsets (ISIC 2020, ISIC 2019, HAM10000) were used for evaluation/testing.
use.other: The individual source datasets (ISIC 2020, ISIC 2019, HAM10000) were used as external test sets to evaluate the model's performance on specific benchmarks.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and F1 score for both binary and multiclass classification tasks.
To evaluate the model's ability to distinguish between classes by plotting the True Positive Rate (TPR) against the False Positive Rate (FPR) and calculating the Area Under the Curve (AUC) score.
To aggregate performance metrics (precision, recall, F1 score) across multiple classes in multiclass classification, accounting for class imbalance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, AUC, F1 score, sensitivity, and specificity without statistical significance testing.

multiclass_classification.notes: Micro average and weighted average F1 scores are highest for this dataset, but macro F1 score is low due to high class imbalance.

binary_classification.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest accuracy and AUC score among compared models on this dataset.

multiclass_classification.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest multiclass classification accuracy (95.49%) among compared models. It also achieved the highest macro F1 score of 0.92.

binary_classification.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest AUC score of 0.99 in binary classification on this dataset.

multiclass_classification.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest accuracy (94.80%) among compared state-of-the-art models on this dataset.

binary_classification.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest performance metrics in binary classification on this dataset.

Training_Knowledge_Base.notes: EfficientNetV2-M achieved higher training accuracy and lower validation loss compared to EfficientNet-B4, though it required more training time.

architecture_used.main_model: Modified EfficientNetV2-M (primary proposed model) and Modified EfficientNet-B4 (baseline comparison)
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning initialized with weights from the ImageNet dataset

architecture_details.modifications: Final fully-connected layer and softmax layer were altered to enable classification into seven distinct classes (AK, BCC, BKL, MEL, NV, SCC, Unknown)
architecture_details.classifier_head: Two dense layers with 500 and 256 neurons respectively, followed by output layers for seven targeted classes

training_details.learning_rate_scheduler: Cosine decay with warm-up epoch of 1
training_details.data_augmentation: Image blurring, distortion, resizing, and normalization
training_details.hardware: Intel core i5 - 9th gen CPU, 16 GB RAM, NVIDIA GTX 1660Ti GPU
training_details.software: Tensorflow v2.6.0, CUDA toolkit 11.4, cuDNN v7.6.5

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation techniques including image blurring, distortion, resizing, and normalization

conclusions: The proposed deep neural network using a modified EfficientNetV2-M architecture outperforms state-of-the-art deep learning-based multiclass classification models for skin cancer detection.
The modified EfficientNetV2-M achieved the highest multiclass classification accuracy of 95.49% on the ISIC 2019 dataset and 94.80% on the HAM10000 dataset.

For binary classification (malignant vs. benign), the EfficientNetV2-M model achieved high accuracy scores of 99.23% on ISIC 2020, 97.06% on ISIC 2019, and 95.95% on HAM10000, with corresponding AUC scores of 0.98, 0.99, and 0.98 respectively.

Aggregating individual multiclass probabilities to perform binary-level classification improves model performance, as the F1 score in binary classification is higher than in multiclass classification.
The model effectively discriminates between malignant and benign features, as validated by Grad-CAM localization maps which provide explainability and insights into the learned features.

While the modified EfficientNetV2-M achieved higher training accuracy and lower validation loss compared to the baseline EfficientNet-B4, it required a longer training time (36 hours vs. 30 hours) and higher computational resources.

The study demonstrates that AI systems can assist dermatologists in early detection and diagnosis, potentially avoiding misdiagnosis caused by human errors and reducing the need for invasive biopsy tests.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant class imbalance, noting that fewer images of malignant lesions were used for training, which may bias the model toward classifying lesions as benign.
The authors suggest that the model's performance on malignant classes could be improved by using images generated by Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) to address the imbalance.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; comparisons rely on descriptive metrics like accuracy and AUC.
Data availability is restricted, with the statement that 'Data will be made available on request,' limiting reproducibility and independent verification.

The model was evaluated on external test sets (ISIC 2020, ISIC 2019, HAM10000) but trained on a combined knowledge base of these datasets, raising potential concerns about data leakage or lack of strict separation between training and testing distributions if not explicitly handled.
The study lacks external validation from independent clinical centers or diverse demographic populations beyond the ISIC/HAM10000 sources, limiting generalizability claims.

The proposed EfficientNetV2-M model requires significantly more computational resources (55 million parameters, 36 hours training time) compared to the baseline EfficientNet-B4 (19 million parameters, 30 hours), which may hinder deployment in resource-constrained settings.

The 'Unknown' class constitutes a large portion of the dataset (27,200 images in the combined knowledge base), and its inclusion in the 'benign' binary class may obscure diagnostic precision for ambiguous lesions.
